etymology.text: Color or defect adjective from the root د ك ن (d k n); compare دَكِنَ (dakina, “to be or become dark-colored”).

body.text: A thing, (S, TA,) [or a garment, (see 1,)] or a horse, (Msb,)
of a blackish colour; of a colour inclining to blackness:
of a colour inclining to that of dust;
of a colour between redness and blackness:
; (Msb, TA;) applied also to a serpent: pl.
, applied also to clouds. (TA.) In the following verse, Lebeed applies it as meaning
A wine-skin that has become in good condition in respect of its colour and odour by reason of its oldness;

A^ugoliY Als~ibaA='a bikul~i A^adokana EaAtiqK
                      
                     
                        A^awo jawonapK qudiHato wafuD~a xitaAmuhaA
I buy wine at a high price, together with every blackish,
wine-jar smeared with pitch, from which one has ladled out, the sealed clay upon its mouth having been broken.
A mess of crumbled bread moistened with broth
having a large quantity of seeds with which it is seasoned:
(K:) [app. because of its colour: but SM says,] as though the said seeds were put one upon another on it. (TA.)
